description: 7 skills to take the team from idea discovery to published newsletter. Each skill is a standalone Claude Code skill that can be invoked by any team member.

brand.tagline: We reverse engineer trending AI products and rebuild what actually matters.
brand.editorial_mantra: Find the hook. Decode the product. Rebuild the core.

skills.name: Idea Scout
skills.description: Scans trending sources (Product Hunt, GitHub Trending, Show HN, X/Twitter viral demos) and produces a shortlist of candidate ideas with signal snapshots. This is the top-of-funnel — it feeds candidates into the Hook Scorer.

outputs.name: idea_shortlist
fields: signal_snapshot (stars, upvotes, engagement count, trend rank)

acceptance_criteria: Returns 5-10 candidates per run
Each candidate has a valid URL and signal snapshot
Covers at least 2 different platforms
Signal data is from the last 72 hours
No duplicates from previous runs

strategy_sections: Hook scoring — examples of high scorers (PH launch, GitHub repo exploding, viral X demo, Show HN thread)
Content pillars — Viral Rebuild sources (PH launches, GitHub repos, X demos, Show HN products)

skills.name: Hook Scorer
skills.description: Takes a candidate idea and runs it through a 3-question fast filter, then a full 8-dimension weighted scoring system (max 62.5). Outputs a score, per-dimension breakdown, and a go/no-go decision.
inputs: Idea name + URL (from Idea Scout output or user-provided)

questions: Would someone click this in under 3 seconds?
Can we rebuild the core in 1-2 focused sessions?
Will the audience learn something reusable?
fast_filter.rule: All 3 must be YES to proceed to full scoring

dimensions.name: Attention Strength
dimensions.question: Will a smart internet-native person care instantly?
dimensions.scale: 1=obscure, 2=mildly interesting niche, 3=decent momentum in one community, 4=clearly trending visibly, 5=breakout with obvious curiosity

dimensions.name: Rebuildability
dimensions.question: Can we recreate the core experience fast enough for content?
dimensions.scale: 1=too complex/infra-heavy, 2=possible but impractical, 3=hard but stripped-down version feasible, 4=feasible with clear scoping, 5=ideal for fast reverse-engineer

dimensions.name: Teachability
dimensions.question: Will rebuilding this naturally teach something valuable?
dimensions.scale: 1=little transferable learning, 2=mostly spectacle, 3=one good lesson, 4=several reusable lessons, 5=deeply instructive while entertaining

dimensions.name: Visual Payoff
dimensions.question: Will the audience see obvious progress and a satisfying end result?
dimensions.scale: 1=mostly backend/invisible, 2=difficult to show, 3=somewhat demo-able, 4=visibly satisfying, 5=highly screen-friendly and thumbnail-friendly

dimensions.name: Reveal Potential
dimensions.question: Is there likely to be a surprising insight?
dimensions.scale: 1=no insight beyond 'we built it', 3=one decent reveal, 5=multiple non-obvious takeaways
examples: The wow factor was mostly UX
The hard part wasn't the AI
This looked magical but was mostly orchestration
The original moat is not where people think

dimensions.name: Originality Safety
dimensions.question: Can you cover this without feeling like a lazy clone?
dimensions.scale: 1=feels derivative, 2=hard to differentiate, 3=possible with careful framing, 4=clearly transformative, 5=easy to make original

dimensions.name: Audience Fit
dimensions.question: Does this fit your audience's identity?

dimensions.name: Format Elasticity
dimensions.question: Can this become multiple content assets (newsletter, video, short clip, tweet thread, repo, community challenge)?

acceptance_criteria: Fast filter runs first — if any answer is NO, returns reject without full scoring
All 8 dimensions scored with reasoning
Weighted total matches formula exactly
Decision maps correctly to threshold bands
Report is scannable in under 30 seconds

skills.name: Pillar Matcher
skills.description: Takes a scored idea and recommends which of the 7 content pillars best fits it. Suggests 3-5 title options using that pillar's patterns. Considers the recommended pillar mix to maintain content variety.
inputs: Previous issues' pillar assignments (to maintain mix)

outputs.name: pillar_recommendation
fields: secondary_pillar (alternative if primary doesn't work out)
suggested_titles (3-5 options following pillar's title patterns)
pillar_mix_status (how this fits the recommended 12-issue distribution)

pillars.name: Viral Rebuild
pillars.core_question: Can we recreate the core experience ourselves?
pillars.best_for: Product Hunt launches, GitHub repos, X demos, Show HN products
title_patterns: We Rebuilt Today's #1 Product Hunt Launch
This AI Demo Went Viral. We Tried to Recreate It
We Reverse Engineered a Trending GitHub AI Tool

pillars.name: Hype Audit
pillars.core_question: Is this actually impressive, or just smart presentation?
title_patterns: Is This Viral AI Product Actually Hard to Build?
We Tested the Hype Behind This Trending AI Tool

pillars.name: Feature Extraction
pillars.core_question: What is the smallest piece that made this product spread?
title_patterns: We Rebuilt the One Feature That Made This App Blow Up
The Real Magic of This AI Product Was Just One Workflow

pillars.name: Better Than the Original
pillars.core_question: What would we change if we built this ourselves?
title_patterns: We Rebuilt This Viral AI Tool — But Made It Better
This Product Was Brilliant, But We Fixed Its Biggest Flaw

pillars.name: Build Under Constraint
pillars.core_question: Can we build this with a specific limitation?
title_patterns: We Had 90 Minutes to Rebuild This AI Product
Could Claude Code Recreate This Viral Tool in One Session?

pillars.name: Open-Source Equivalent
pillars.core_question: What's the simplest useful version anyone can build?
title_patterns: We Built an Open Version of This Popular AI Product
Here's the Lean Version of a Viral AI Startup

pillars.name: Lessons From the Build
pillars.core_question: What patterns keep showing up in winning AI products?
title_patterns: What 10 Viral AI Products Had in Common
The UX Patterns Behind the Best AI Demos
What Rebuilding 12 AI Products Taught Us

acceptance_criteria: Recommends primary + secondary pillar with reasoning
Titles follow the pillar's established patterns
Mix tracking warns if a pillar is overused or neglected
At least 3 title options provided

skills.name: Virality Scorer
skills.description: Scores the story packaging of a piece on 8 dimensions (max 57.5). This is different from hook scoring — a hook can be good for building but still fail as content. If hook score is high but virality score is low, suggests repackaging strategies instead of dropping the idea.
inputs: Idea name + chosen pillar + proposed title
Draft angle/framing (how you plan to present it)

outputs.name: virality_score_report
fields: repackaging_suggestions (if hook high but virality low)

dimensions.name: Instant Curiosity
dimensions.question: Would the title alone trigger 'Wait, what?'?

dimensions.name: Stakes
dimensions.question: Is there a clear consequence or tension (time limit, quality target, blind comparison, constrained toolset)?
dimensions.note: Low virality often comes from low stakes

dimensions.name: Novelty
dimensions.question: Does it feel fresh, not like generic AI content?
high_novelty_examples: We tested whether the hype was fake
We isolated the feature that made this blow up

dimensions.name: Visual Promise
dimensions.question: Can the thumbnail/hero image tell a story fast?

dimensions.name: Shareability
dimensions.question: Would someone send this to a founder, builder friend, colleague, or AI enthusiast?
high_share_reasons: You should see how simple this actually was
This breakdown explains why this product spread
This is how you'd build it

dimensions.name: Identity Pull
dimensions.question: Does this make the viewer feel like an insider, smart builder, early adopter, or someone who understands what's actually happening?

dimensions.name: Clipability
dimensions.question: Can you extract 3-5 short moments?
clip_examples: The hardest part had nothing to do with AI
This one feature made the whole product work

options: Compare your version against the original

acceptance_criteria: All 8 dimensions scored with reasoning
Weighted total matches formula
Verdict maps to correct threshold band
Repackaging suggestions trigger automatically when hook high + virality low
Clearly distinguishes between hook quality and packaging quality in the report

skills.name: Newsletter Writer
skills.description: Writes the full 10-section newsletter draft following the template. Takes all prior skill outputs (scored idea, pillar, title, virality assessment) plus the team's build notes/logs, and produces a complete draft with visual callouts marked inline.
inputs: Build notes/logs (attempts, failures, pivots, final result)

outputs.name: newsletter_draft
sections: The Hook — 3-5 lines: what happened, why it got attention, what challenge you're taking on
The Object — compact profile card (name, source, why trending, value prop, signal snapshot) + [VISUAL: hero screenshot, signal card]
Why It Worked — 3 subsections: what users saw, what made it feel magical, what drove virality + [VISUAL: annotated screenshot, magic breakdown diagram]
The Rebuild Brief — objective, constraints, tool stack, success criteria, what you're ignoring + [VISUAL: challenge card, feature checklist]
How We Think It Works — probable inputs, model/workflow, orchestration, UX layer, hidden complexity + [VISUAL: architecture diagram, flowchart]
The Build Log — attempt progression with failures and pivots + [VISUAL: iteration screenshots, before/after, debugging snapshots]
Final Comparison — original vs rebuilt, what matched, what didn't, harder/easier than expected + [VISUAL: side-by-side, feature matrix, scorecard]
The Real Lesson — 3-5 sharp transferable lessons + [VISUAL: framework card, reusable checklist]
Build Takeaway — reusable asset (prompt template, mini architecture, starter repo, workflow, pattern) + [VISUAL: template preview, cheat sheet]
Next Week's Tease — open loop for return rate

voice_guidelines.tone: Premium field report, not a blog post
voice_guidelines.code_rule: Don't add too much raw code. Show code only when it teaches a specific decision.
subject_line_patterns: We Rebuilt Today's #1 Product Hunt Launch
This AI Demo Went Viral. Here's How We'd Build It
We Tried to Recreate a Trending GitHub Project
The Real Trick Behind This Viral AI Product

acceptance_criteria: All 10 sections present and populated (11 including subject line)
Visual callouts marked inline for every section that needs them
Subject line follows mission-driven pattern, not generic blog title
Opening hook is 3-5 lines max
Build log shows progression (attempts, failures, pivots) — feels alive, not over-polished
Lessons are transferable, not just 'we built it'
Includes a reusable asset the reader can take away
Next week tease creates an open loop
No large code dumps — code only when it teaches a decision

strategy_sections: Recommended visual system for the newsletter
Suggested one-issue template in compact form

skills.name: Visual Asset Planner
skills.description: Reads a newsletter draft and generates a per-section shot list of all required images and artifacts. Categorizes each as must-have or nice-to-have. Every visual must prove it, explain it, or compare it — no decorative filler.

outputs.name: visual_asset_plan
fields_per_asset: section (which newsletter section it belongs to)
purpose (prove it / explain it / compare it)

must_have_types: Signal card — upvotes, stars, engagement, ranking, trend source
Annotated teardown screenshot — arrows, labels, highlighted interaction moments
Build brief card — goal, constraints, success criteria
Architecture diagram — simple inferred system flow
Final side-by-side comparison — original vs rebuilt
nice_to_have_types: Harder than expected / easier than expected split card
rules: Every visual must do one of three jobs: prove it, explain it, or compare it
No huge code dumps as images
No decorative visuals with no informational value
acceptance_criteria: Every newsletter section has at least one visual assigned
All 8 must-have types are present in the plan
Each asset tagged with purpose (prove/explain/compare)
No decorative-only visuals included
Specs are actionable enough for a designer or image generator to execute

strategy_sections: Recommended visual system for the newsletter

skills.name: Newsletter Reviewer
skills.description: Final quality gate before publishing. Reviews the complete newsletter draft + visual plan against template completeness, brand voice, the 5-point core content test, and visual system compliance. Returns a pass/revise verdict with specific, actionable feedback.
inputs: Visual asset plan (from Visual Asset Planner)

outputs.name: review_report
fields: brand_voice_check (field report tone, not blog post — pass/fail with notes)
visual_system_check (must-haves present, no decorative filler — pass/fail)
strengths (what's working well — so the writer doesn't break it)
revision_priority (ordered list of what to fix first)

required_sections: The Rebuild Brief (goal, constraints, stack, success, ignoring)
How We Think It Works (system breakdown)

core_content_test: Strong external hook — is the source object genuinely trending?
Clear challenge — is the rebuild brief specific and compelling?
Visible progress — does the build log show real iteration?
Satisfying comparison — is original vs rebuilt genuinely insightful?
Transferable lesson — will the reader walk away smarter?

brand_voice.must_not_feel_like: Blog post, tutorial, or generic AI content
check_points: Subject line sounds like a mission, not a newsletter
Opening hook is 3-5 lines, not a paragraph
Build log feels alive, not polished to death
Code shown only when it teaches a decision
Lessons are sharp and transferable, not generic

acceptance_criteria: Checks all 10+1 sections for presence and quality
Runs all 5 core content test checks
Brand voice check catches blog-post-ish tone
Visual system check validates must-have coverage
Feedback is section-specific and actionable (not vague)
Strengths are called out so good work isn't accidentally undone
Revision priorities are ordered by impact
